text: I'm interested in something more than a nudge, a very simple, low, gentle nudge, because I don't think it's going to produce a lot of change. I'd be interested in something more like 33% of the fee. It's not quite any of the options that we have up there, sort of cutting the middle between the 20 and 50. But I think that that gets us to a place where we're getting some, a meaningful nudge to move us in the right direction. But it's kind of considers the affordability concerns that are coming up in the community. I think the other big thing I would say about affordability is that I honestly think that this is being framed as a false binary choice between an either or. You either get some action on climate change or you protect affordability and that it's impossible to get both of those outcomes. And I really just don't think that that's a viable, I don't think it's the right framing. I think it's inaccurate. I think it's false. And frankly, I think it's being used to scare people and kind of a bit of a boogeyman. I've seen this in a variety of other settings throughout my life, often around environmental issues before I moved to Bend and Seattle. And most of the research and stuff that I saw about those issues in the past showed that generally those binary choices are not actually how things work in the real world and as you implement stuff. And so I just want to call that out. I think that we can, I think as one commenter said in an email to us, we can walk and chew gum. We can protect affordability in our community. That's mostly about supply, but it's also about demand in our community. We have a lot of people who want to be in this wonderful place that we live, and it's those two pieces together that are driving affordability. We've done a lot. We will continue. This council is very committed to doing that going forward. I think we can do that, and we can take some action on, meaningful action on reducing our use of fossil fuels. So, but I would support that 33% goal. Okay. Ordering off menu. All right. It's all right. It's what we're here for. Go ahead. Councilor Meadows, I'm enthusiastic by your response and especially bringing up the transportation. And when you say that, what comes to my mind is the 53% of people that drive in to Bend every day to go to work because they cannot afford to live here. So that is something that is on the top of my mind that I would, and if we want to do something about the environment, getting people to live in the city where they work and be able to get to work, walk or bike or whatever to work, is to me the number one thing that we can do on this council. I am really struggling. It's kind of come as zero surprise to any of you, but I'm really struggling with this. And I'm struggling with a program that is, it kind of seems to me like we're robbing Peter to pay Paul. And especially
